verses.text: Joash does what is right all the days Jehoiada the priest instructs him, though the high places are not removed. His goodness leans heavily on a godly mentor. Faith borrowed from another's strength is real, yet must one day become our own.

Joash does what is right all the days Jehoiada the priest instructs him, though the high places are not removed. His goodness leans heavily on a godly mentor. Faith borrowed from another's strength is real, yet must one day become our own.

Joash does what is right all the days Jehoiada the priest instructs him, though the high places are not removed. His goodness leans heavily on a godly mentor. Faith borrowed from another's strength is real, yet must one day become our own.

Joash orders the priests to repair the temple from the people's offerings, but by his twenty-third year the work is still undone. Good intentions stall without follow-through. Even a worthy cause can languish when no one is truly accountable for getting it done.

Joash orders the priests to repair the temple from the people's offerings, but by his twenty-third year the work is still undone. Good intentions stall without follow-through. Even a worthy cause can languish when no one is truly accountable for getting it done.

Jehoiada sets up a chest by the altar; the people give gladly, and the money goes straight to the workmen for repairs. A simple, honest system revives the work. When giving is direct and trust is kept, the house of God is restored.

Jehoiada sets up a chest by the altar; the people give gladly, and the money goes straight to the workmen for repairs. A simple, honest system revives the work. When giving is direct and trust is kept, the house of God is restored.

The workmen deal so faithfully that no accounting is required of them. Their integrity needs no audit. The highest commendation of a servant is that he can be trusted with what is not watched, handling holy things with a clean conscience.

When Hazael threatens Jerusalem, Joash strips the temple and his own treasures to buy him off. The king who restored God's house now empties it in fear. Faith that once built can falter, trusting silver to save where it should have trusted God.

When Hazael threatens Jerusalem, Joash strips the temple and his own treasures to buy him off. The king who restored God's house now empties it in fear. Faith that once built can falter, trusting silver to save where it should have trusted God.

Joash is murdered by his own servants and buried, ending a reign that began so well. The promise of his youth dims in his later years. A good start does not guarantee a good end; we must walk with God to the last, not only at the first.
